data.title: Kabuki-Za (TSE:9661) Q3 EPS Slowdown Challenges Bullish Earnings Momentum Narrative
data.description:
Kabuki-Za (TSE:9661) has released its Q3 2026 numbers with revenue of ¥905 million and basic EPS of ¥5.78, set against a backdrop of 36.9% earnings growth over the past year and a trailing net profit margin of 10.9%. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from ¥746 million in Q3 2025 to ¥905 million in Q3 2026. Over the same period, trailing twelve month EPS has shifted from ¥23.76 to ¥31.52, giving a clearer sense of how the top line and per share profitability have evolved through the year. Taken together, the latest quarter comes on top of a year where margins have firmed up, which is likely to be a key point of focus for investors as they assess this update.

See our full analysis for Kabuki-Za.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the prevailing narratives around Kabuki-Za to see which views are reinforced by the data and which ones start to look stretched.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Margins Backed by ¥382m LTM Profit

  • Over the last twelve months, Kabuki-Za booked ¥382 million of net income on ¥3,510 million of revenue, tying directly to the 10.9% net margin highlighted earlier.
  • Supporters of a bullish view point to this profit base together with 36.9% earnings growth over the year and a five year annualized earnings growth rate of 70.7%. They see this as evidence of a business that is now firmly generating profits. However, the very high trailing P/E of 149.8x means that, even with higher margins than last year’s 9%, the market is already assigning a premium price to that progress.
    • The shift from last year’s 9% to 10.9% net margin aligns with the bullish idea of improving efficiency, but it sits against a much lower industry P/E of 12.5x.
    • The trailing twelve month EPS of ¥31.52 and the move to profitability over five years line up with the positive narrative, while also showing why expectations embedded in the current share price are set at a high bar.

Earnings Pattern Across Recent Quarters

  • Within 2026, basic EPS moved from ¥5.28 in Q1 to ¥10.07 in Q2 and then ¥5.78 in Q3, while revenue ranged from ¥853 million to ¥935 million over the same period.
  • Investors taking a cautious, more bearish angle may focus on how net income shifted between ¥64 million, ¥122 million, and ¥70 million over these three quarters, arguing that profits are not moving in a straight line. At the same time, the trailing twelve month totals still sum to ¥382 million of net income and ¥3,510 million of revenue, which gives a fuller picture than any single period.
    • Q2 2026 net income of ¥122 million compared with Q3’s ¥70 million provides the numbers that bears can point to when they question earnings consistency.
    • At the same time, trailing twelve month EPS rising from ¥22.61 in Q4 2025 to ¥31.52 in Q3 2026 shows that the broader period still reflects growth even where individual quarters differ.

Premium P/E vs 12.5x Industry

  • Kabuki-Za trades on a trailing P/E of 149.8x, compared with a peer average of 128.9x and a JP Real Estate industry average of 12.5x. The current share price of ¥4,720 also sits well above the DCF fair value of ¥646.62.
  • What stands out for a bearish narrative is the wide gap between that ¥4,720 price tag and the ¥646.62 DCF fair value, together with the high P/E. This suggests the market is paying a large premium relative to both peers and the industry even though the underlying net margin is 10.9% and trailing twelve month net income is ¥382 million.
    • The difference between the 149.8x P/E and the 12.5x industry average is more than a simple premium and gives skeptics a clear numerical anchor for valuation concerns.
    • The spread between the current share price and DCF fair value indicates that any future change in earnings or margins could have an outsized effect on how this valuation is viewed.
Skeptics point to the 149.8x P/E and the gap to ¥646.62 DCF fair value as reasons to stress test this story further, especially now that margins sit at 10.9% and earnings have recently grown 36.9%, yet expectations remain high. 📊 Read the full Kabuki-Za Consensus Narrative.

data.title: Sala (TSE:2734) Earnings Growth Of 11.8% Tests Market Discount Narrative
data.description:
Sala (TSE:2734) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of ¥64.6 billion and basic EPS of ¥13.00, alongside trailing twelve month EPS of ¥91.43 and net income of ¥5,870 million that reflect the latest run rate in earnings power. Over recent periods, the company has seen quarterly revenue move between ¥55.7 billion and ¥65.9 billion with EPS ranging from ¥13.00 to ¥37.01, while trailing net profit margin sat at 2.3% compared with 2.2% a year earlier. This gives a clear read on how effectively sales are translating into profit. Taken together, these results highlight a business where modest margin shifts, rather than headline revenue swings, are likely to be the key focus for investors dissecting this update.

See our full analysis for SALA.

With the latest figures on the table, the next step is to see how these earnings line up with the main stories investors follow about Sala, and where the numbers push back against those narratives.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Earnings Growth Anchored By 11.8% Trailing Gain

  • Over the last 12 months Sala generated earnings growth of 11.8%, with five year earnings compounding at 7.4% per year and trailing net profit of ¥5,870 million on ¥251,533 million of revenue, which backs up the current trailing EPS of ¥91.43.
  • What stands out for a bullish take is that these profit numbers sit alongside a modest 2.3% net margin, which is only slightly above last year’s 2.2%, so:
    • Supporters who focus on earnings growth can point to both the 11.8% trailing increase and the 7.4% five year annualized rate as evidence that Sala has been adding profit on top of a relatively steady margin base.
    • On the other hand, the small margin shift means the bullish case is leaning more on consistent profit generation than on any big improvement in efficiency, because the revenue base over the last year of ¥251,533 million produced a margin level that is close to the prior year.
In light of this mix of steady margins and 11.8% earnings growth, some investors will want to see how a full bull and bear debate frames Sala’s profit story over the last five years. 📊 Read the full SALA Consensus Narrative.

P/E Of 11.9x Versus Higher Peer Averages

  • Sala trades on a P/E of 11.9x compared with a peer average of 15.6x and an Asian Oil & Gas industry average of 13.8x, so the shares are priced below both of those comparison points on trailing earnings.
  • Supporters arguing a bullish valuation angle see this relative discount as backed by the current fundamentals, and the numbers create a clear tension with a more cautious stance:
    • On one side, the combination of 11.8% trailing earnings growth and 7.4% five year earnings growth with a below peer P/E can be read as the market asking for a lower multiple despite that track record.
    • On the other side, more cautious investors can point out that Sala’s net margin is 2.3% which is only slightly above 2.2% a year earlier, so the lower P/E may reflect the business operating on thin margins rather than an obvious mispricing.

DCF Gap And Unstable Dividend Record

  • The DCF fair value in the dataset is ¥1,750.63 against a current share price of ¥1,086, which implies the shares trade about 38% below that modelled value, while the dividend track record is described as unstable.
  • Critics who focus on risks often highlight that income reliability matters as much as headline value signals, and Sala’s numbers give both sides something to point to:
    • Bears can argue that an unstable dividend history makes it harder for income focused holders to rely on cash returns, even though the business produced ¥5,870 million in trailing net income and EPS of ¥91.43.
    • Supporters of the DCF view can counter that, despite the dividend description, the combination of earnings growth, a 2.3% net margin and a price that is about 38% below the ¥1,750.63 DCF fair value presents a clear valuation gap at the current ¥1,086 share price.

data.title: Does LendingClub (LC) Still Offer Upside After A 31% One Year Share Price Gain?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether LendingClub's share price still lines up with its fundamentals, you are not alone. This article focuses squarely on what the current price might imply about value.
  • With the stock at US$20.16 and returns of 0.9% over 7 days, 1.3% over 30 days, 5.4% year to date, 30.7% over 1 year and 99.6% over 3 years, many investors are asking how much of the story is already in the price.

These themes help frame why the current share price and past returns are getting a closer look from investors assessing risk and opportunity.

LendingClub scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: LendingClub Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much value a company can create above the return that shareholders are asking for. Instead of focusing on near term earnings alone, it compares LendingClub’s expected profitability on its equity base to the cost of that equity.

For LendingClub, book value is $12.68 per share and the stable book value estimate is $16.26 per share, based on weighted future book value estimates from 6 analysts. The model uses a stable EPS of $2.23 per share, sourced from weighted future return on equity estimates from the same 6 analysts, and an average return on equity of 13.70%.

The cost of equity is $1.27 per share, so the model estimates an excess return of $0.96 per share, which is the value created after covering that equity cost. When those excess returns are projected forward and discounted back, the model arrives at an intrinsic value of about $37.31 per share.

Compared with the current price of US$20.16, this implies the stock is 46.0% undervalued according to this approach.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: LendingClub Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like LendingClub, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties the share price directly to the business’s ability to generate profit, which is what ultimately supports shareholder returns over time.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how investors view growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower one. LendingClub currently trades on a P/E of 22.38x, compared with the Consumer Finance industry average of 9.01x and a peer average of 6.14x, so the stock is priced at a higher earnings multiple than these broad groups.

data.description:

Apple scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Apple Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model looks at the cash Apple is expected to generate in the future and discounts those projected cash flows back to today to estimate what the company might be worth now.

For Apple, the model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about US$99.9b. Analysts have provided free cash flow estimates for the next few years, and Simply Wall St extrapolates this further, with projected free cash flow of US$184.1b in 2030. All of these future cash flows are discounted back to today using an appropriate required return, then summed to give a total equity value.

On this basis, the estimated intrinsic value from the DCF is US$226.33 per share, compared with the recent share price of US$261.05. That gap implies the stock is about 15.3% overvalued according to this particular model.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Apple Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Apple, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it links what you pay per share to the earnings the company is already generating. It gives you a quick sense of how much the market is paying for each dollar of profit.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends a lot on how fast earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower one.

Apple currently trades on a P/E of 34.25x. That compares with a Tech industry average P/E of 21.62x and a peer average of 33.18x, so the shares sit above the wider industry and slightly above peers. Sometimes these stories can cloud the minds of investors, leading them to invest with their emotions rather than on the merit of good company fundamentals. Loss making companies can act like a sponge for capital - so investors should be cautious that they're not throwing good money after bad.

So if this idea of high risk and high reward doesn't suit, you might be more interested in profitable, growing companies, like SRG Global (ASX:SRG). Now this is not to say that the company presents the best investment opportunity around, but profitability is a key component to success in business.

How Quickly Is SRG Global Increasing Earnings Per Share?

If you believe that markets are even vaguely efficient, then over the long term you'd expect a company's share price to follow its earnings per share (EPS) outcomes. If growth like this continues on into the future, then shareholders will have plenty to smile about.

It's often helpful to take a look at earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) margins, as well as revenue growth, to get another take on the quality of the company's growth. What Does The Institutional Ownership Tell Us About Centuria Capital Group?

Institutional investors commonly compare their own returns to the returns of a commonly followed index. So they generally do consider buying larger companies that are included in the relevant benchmark index.

As you can see, institutional investors have a fair amount of stake in Centuria Capital Group. This implies the analysts working for those institutions have looked at the stock and they like it. But just like anyone else, they could be wrong. If multiple institutions change their view on a stock at the same time, you could see the share price drop fast. It's therefore worth looking at Centuria Capital Group's earnings history below. Of course, the future is what really matters.

Centuria Capital Group is not owned by hedge funds. The Vanguard Group, Inc. is currently the company's largest shareholder with 9.2% of shares outstanding. For context, the second largest shareholder holds about 6.0% of the shares outstanding, followed by an ownership of 5.0% by the third-largest shareholder. Furthermore, CEO John McBain is the owner of 1.0% of the company's shares.

A deeper look at our ownership data shows that the top 25 shareholders collectively hold less than half of the register, suggesting a large group of small holders where no single shareholder has a majority.

While studying institutional ownership for a company can add value to your research, it is also a good practice to research analyst recommendations to get a deeper understand of a stock's expected performance. There are plenty of analysts covering the stock, so it might be worth seeing what they are forecasting, too.

Insider Ownership Of Centuria Capital Group

The definition of an insider can differ slightly between different countries, but members of the board of directors always count. Management ultimately answers to the board. However, it is not uncommon for managers to be executive board members, especially if they are a founder or the CEO.

Most consider insider ownership a positive because it can indicate the board is well aligned with other shareholders. However, on some occasions too much power is concentrated within this group.

We can see that insiders own shares in Centuria Capital Group. The insiders have a meaningful stake worth AU$115m. Most would see this as a real positive. Most would say this shows alignment of interests between shareholders and the board. Still, it might be worth checking if those insiders have been selling.

General Public Ownership

The general public-- including retail investors -- own 49% stake in the company, and hence can't easily be ignored. This size of ownership, while considerable, may not be enough to change company policy if the decision is not in sync with other large shareholders.

Private Company Ownership

It seems that Private Companies own 7.6%, of the Centuria Capital Group stock. It might be worth looking deeper into this.
